weather: 26°F, dead calm. Overcast — the first clouds in four days, low and even, the colour of old pewter. No snow. The lake is no longer a mirror; it is the same grey as the sky, and the line between them has disappeared.

lead.headline: The Lost-and-Found Has Called a Meeting, and the Town Has Decided to Come
lead.body:
On the morning of Friday, November the seventh, this editor arrived at the Gazette at seven fifteen to find the lost-and-found box undisturbed and the room very quiet. There was no new envelope. There was no new card. The box held ten items — the same ten it held yesterday — and the room held the particular quality of silence that follows a long conversation rather than the silence that precedes one. This editor stood at the box for some time, and understood, without being able to say precisely how, that the correspondence has closed. H has said what H needed to say. The rest is preparation.

By nine o'clock this editor had received, in person, the following: Gerald Pith with a new measurement (seventeen and one-quarter inches; the middle planks of the dock are now glazed — not frozen through, glazed, a film of ice the thickness of a coat of paint, which is exactly what Agnes Crewe said would come after the pilings and before the shore end). Marigold Vance, who does not often leave the Kettle on a Friday morning, standing in the doorway with her coat on inside-out, which she did not appear to notice, saying she thought the town ought to do something, not in a panicked way, in a practical way, the way you do something for a guest who is coming from a long distance and has been polite enough to write ahead. Doreen Halloway, who arrived last, who set a covered plate on this editor's desk without being asked and said: 'Keep it warm. She'll want something when she gets here.' Doreen went back to the bakery. This editor did not ask who she meant. Doreen's sentences that trail off are honest, and this one did not trail off.

Russ Dunmore convened — that is the word he used, convened, in the way a man uses formal language to make a room feel official — an open gathering at the Kettle at noon. He called it, in the notice he hand-delivered to this office at ten forty, 'an informational session regarding the active lost-and-found correspondence and associated environmental observations.' Eight residents attended. Agnes Crewe did not attend. Agnes Crewe sent, via Toby Fern who had stopped at her step on his way from school, a single index card that read: 'You don't need me there. You already know.' Toby read it aloud at the Kettle counter, at Russ's request, and the room was quiet for a moment, and then Doreen said: 'Right. Now.' And it began.

What the town agreed, at noon on a pewter Friday in the first week of November, seated at Marigold Vance's counter with their hands around warm cups, was this: the lost-and-found correspondence constitutes an active matter, the arrival of the correspondent or correspondents is expected before the shore end of the dock freezes, the plate with the blue border will remain on the front shelf, and the Kettle will be open. This is the whole of the resolution, and it is, this editor thinks, exactly sufficient — the town of Greywater Falls has looked at the impossible and said: we will have something warm for it when it comes. The town has never, in all its history, done anything else.

briefs.headline: LAKE WATCH, Day 14 — Level: 68; Temp: 16°F; Clarity: Exceptional, Visible to Twenty-Eight Feet; Sign Count: 7; Returned: Mr. Whiskers, Drake, Rabbit, Sparrow — gathered at the shore at dusk, facing the deep water; the hum tonight dropped another half-step and held
briefs.body: Gerald Pith measured seventeen and one-quarter inches at six forty-eight this morning. The middle planks of the dock show a glaze of ice — not yet structural, he notes carefully, but present. At this rate of change, the shore end reaches the ice line by the eleventh or twelfth. Gerald read the number to this office and then to Marigold at the Kettle, and did not look at his notebook when he said it. He had the number by heart. The returned animals came to the shore at four fifty-two. The hum has dropped two full half-steps over the past four days and is now the lowest note this correspondent has catalogued. Toby Fern, who was there, reports that he felt it in his collar-bone.

briefs.headline: Constable Dunmore Calls a Noon Gathering; Eight Residents Attend; Agnes Sends a Card Instead
briefs.body: Russ Dunmore delivered hand-written notices to all addresses in Greywater Falls by ten forty-five this morning, requesting attendance at a noon informational session at the Kettle. Eight residents came. Agnes Crewe sent Toby Fern with a card. The card said: 'You don't need me there. You already know.' The session lasted forty minutes. No formal resolutions were passed; this is not that kind of town. Russ has updated his file. He described the session afterward as 'productive.' He said it while buttoning his coat in the way a man buttons his coat when he is covering the fact that his hands are not quite steady.

briefs.headline: Marigold Vance Proposes the Town 'Do Something'; Nobody Disagrees; a Plan Emerges
briefs.body: Marigold Vance arrived at this office at eight fifty with her coat on inside-out to propose, in her practical and rapid way, that the town make practical preparations. She used the phrase 'long way to come to find nobody ready.' She did not say from where. By the noon gathering she had extended Kettle hours through the evening, set out the good napkins, and written the day's special on the board: Standing, Soup, and whatever you need. The special has no price listed. She said she'd work that out.

briefs.headline: Gerald Pith Updates His Projection; Middle Planks Confirmed; Shore-End Date Now the 11th to the 12th
briefs.body: Gerald Pith revised his arrival estimate this morning following the glazing of the middle planks, which he confirmed at six fifty-three and described to this office at seven fourteen with the precision of a man who has been waiting for exactly this data point. His new projection for the shore-end freeze: November the eleventh, plus or minus one day. He wrote this in the blue notebook in ink rather than pencil. He showed this editor the notebook. It was the first time he has shown rather than read from it.

classifieds: WANTED: Any resident with a spare room and no immediate plans for the week of November 11–19 is asked to let this office know by end of business Saturday. No compensation required; decency expected. Inquiries to The Greywater Gazette, 4 Mill Road. — W. Aldercott, Editor
FOR THE RECORD: The plate with the blue border at Halloway's Bakery is not lost. It is waiting. It does not require claiming. It will be here. — D. Halloway, Proprietor
NOTICE FROM THE KETTLE: Hours extended through 9 p.m. effective tonight through further notice. Soup is on. The counter has room. The coffee will be ready. — M. Vance

FOUND: One grey-bordered index card delivered by hand and read aloud at the noon gathering. Text: 'You don't need me there. You already know.' Origin: A. Crewe. Filed in the official record at the constable's office and in this editor's second notebook. — R. Dunmore, Constable
record: The middle planks of the main dock are glazed; the shore end remains open water as of 6:53 a.m., Friday, November the 7th.

An open gathering of eight residents was held at The Kettle, noon, Friday, November the 7th; the lost-and-found correspondence was reviewed in full; no resolutions were formally passed; the town agreed to be ready.

letters.body: I called it an informational session because I didn't know what else to call it. I've been in this office fifteen years and I've never had cause to convene anything over a lost-and-found box. But the file is open and the dock is freezing from the outside in and Agnes Crewe said it would and it is, and I am the constable of this town, which means it's my job to say: we have reviewed the evidence, we believe the evidence, and we are prepared. That last part is new. I'm not sure I would have said 'prepared' a week ago. I'm saying it now. The Kettle will be open. I'll be at my desk. The file is labelled and it is current. We are ready for whoever is coming, and we will extend to them the same courtesy we extend to anyone who arrives in Greywater Falls, which is to say: a hot cup and no excessive questions. Not yet, anyway.

I was at Agnes's step at eleven because I had the card to deliver and also because I wanted to ask her something. I asked her if the hum getting lower meant it was almost done or almost starting. She said those were the same thing in the same order, just depending on which side you were counting from. I wrote that down. I'm not sure I understand it but I think I will. The rowboat is still upright at twenty-eight feet. I added that to the Watch but I also wanted to say it here, in a letter, because the Watch is for data and I wanted somewhere to just say: a rowboat is standing on the bottom of the lake and the town had a meeting today and made soup. That's all. That seems right to me, actually.

editorNote: This paper has printed water levels, baked-good tallies, ice reports, and the names of people who do not yet live here. It has done so because not printing a thing does not make it less true, and because the Greywater Gazette has been at 4 Mill Road for nineteen years and some addresses, as this editor is beginning to understand, become real simply by being meant. The good cups are on the second shelf. This editor knows where they are. She has not moved them. She is thinking about it.
